items.correct: The level of output where total revenue equals total costs — neither profit nor loss. It is NOT the level where profit is maximised; profit grows beyond break-even.
items.misconception: Break-even is where the business makes the most profit.
items.random_wrong: Break-even is where the business owes no debt.
items.why: Break-even is a no-profit-no-loss point, useful for risk analysis. Students conflate it with profit maximisation. Going above break-even is where profit starts; below break-even means a loss.

items.q: What is the difference between fixed and variable costs?
items.correct: Fixed costs do not change with output level (e.g. rent, salaries); variable costs change in proportion to output (e.g. raw materials, hourly wages).
items.misconception: Fixed costs are paid every month and variable costs are paid weekly.
items.random_wrong: Fixed costs are the cheapest costs and variable costs are the most expensive.
items.why: The distinction is about behaviour with output, not payment frequency. Rent is fixed because producing more units doesn't increase it; flour for a bakery is variable because more loaves means more flour.

items.q: What is the difference between profit and revenue?
items.correct: Revenue is total income from sales (price x quantity sold); profit is revenue minus total costs — what is left after paying for everything.
items.misconception: Profit and revenue are different words for the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Profit is what businesses earn; revenue is what employees earn.
items.why: A business can have huge revenue but make no profit if costs are equally huge. Conflating them leads to nonsensical analysis — Amazon had high revenue but low profits for years.
Correct answer: Actual sales − break-even sales. “Break-even units” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If contribution per unit is £5 and you sell 200 units past break-even, profit is.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: £1,000. “£100” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Fixed costs £12,000. Price £20. Variable cost £14. What is the break-even output, in units?
items.why: Exact answer: 2000. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
